title: Will future biomedical advances be low marginal cost? - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Cowen argues that as biomedicine shifts from low-marginal-cost pills to genome-personalized, high-marginal-cost treatments, low-pay procurement systems like Britain's NHS lose their advantage and well-capitalized 'overspending' systems like the US adapt more easily.

insights.claim: Most pharmaceuticals carry high upfront discovery/testing costs and near-zero marginal costs, which lets low-pay systems like Britain's extract a good deal through price discrimination.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Explains why single-payer systems negotiate hard successfully today — a structural advantage tied to the current cost curve.
insights.quote: That cost structure favors health systems, such as that of Britain, that try to pay lower for services.
insights.locator: Most pharmaceuticals involve high upfront costs

insights.claim: A future of genome-sequenced, personalized treatments will likely carry relatively high marginal costs, eroding the price-discrimination advantage of low-pay procurement systems.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: A structural shift that would degrade single-payer leverage — relevant to pharma pricing power and where future biomedical value accrues.
insights.quote: very likely those remedies will have relatively high marginal costs

insights.claim: Well-capitalized, 'overspending' health systems like the US will have an easier time adjusting to high-marginal-cost personalized medicine, a neglected rising relative advantage.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Inverts the usual critique of US health spending — a contrarian macro view that high-capex systems win in the next biomedical regime.
insights.quote: The rising relative advantage of well-capitalized health care systems
